title: Saltimbocca alla Romana
subtitle: Roman veal escalopes with Parma ham and sage, pan-fried in white wine and butter
description: Saltimbocca — 'jump in the mouth' — is one of Rome's most beloved trattoria dishes. Thin veal escalopes are layered with a sage leaf and a slice of Parma ham, secured with a wooden cocktail stick, then pan-fried quickly in butter and finished with white wine. The whole dish takes under fifteen minutes once the escalopes are flattened. The sauce is not a long reduction but a rapid deglaze — sharp, buttery, and brief.
sourceNotes: Saltimbocca alla romana is one of the canonical dishes of Roman cuisine. Ada Boni's Il Talismano della Felicità (1927) contains an early recipe; the dish is also described in Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina (1891). It is a fixture of Roman trattoria menus.

recipeTools.notes: Wide enough to hold all the escalopes in a single layer — 28–30 cm. If the pan is too small, cook in two batches.
Or a rolling pin — for flattening the escalopes to an even 5 mm.
To secure the ham and sage to the veal.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin, boneless slice of meat — usually veal, chicken, or pork — beaten flat to an even thickness to ensure quick, even cooking.
Adding liquid (wine, stock, or water) to a hot pan after cooking to dissolve the browned residue (fond) into a quick sauce.

ingredients.notes: About 80g each — ask the butcher for scaloppine, or buy veal leg steaks and flatten them yourself
Go easy — the ham is already salty

steps: If the veal is thick, place each escalope between two sheets of cling film and beat with a meat mallet or rolling pin to an even 5 mm thickness.
Lay a sage leaf on the smooth (outer) side of each escalope. Place a slice of Parma ham on top and secure with a wooden cocktail stick passed through all three layers.
Season the veal side (not the ham side) lightly with salt and pepper. Dust the veal side only with a little flour, shaking off any excess.
Melt 30g of butter in a large frying pan over a medium-high heat until foaming. Place the escalopes ham-side down first. Cook for 2 minutes until the ham is crisp and beginning to brown at the edges.
Turn with tongs and cook the veal side for 1–2 minutes until cooked through. The escalopes cook fast — do not overcook. Transfer to a warm plate.

Pour the white wine into the hot pan and let it bubble vigorously, scraping up any fond, for 1–2 minutes until reduced by half. Add the remaining 30g of butter and swirl the pan to emulsify it into the wine.
Remove the cocktail sticks. Spoon the pan sauce over the escalopes and serve immediately.

content.text: This dish is faster than almost anything else in the Italian repertoire. Have everything ready before the pan goes on — the
assembled, the flour ready, a warm plate beside the hob. Once the butter is in the pan the whole thing takes under eight minutes, and delays mid-cook will leave the meat overdone.

Flatten each of {{veal-escalopes}} of veal escalopes to 5 mm between cling film if needed. Lay a sage leaf on the smooth side of each escalope, place a slice of Parma ham over the top, and secure with a cocktail stick. Season the veal side lightly with salt and pepper and dust with a little flour, shaking off the excess.

Melt 30g of {{butter-unsalted}} of butter in a large frying pan over a medium-high heat. Once foaming, lay the escalopes ham-side down. Cook for 2 minutes until the ham is crisp. Turn and cook the veal side for 1–2 minutes. Transfer to a warm plate.
Pour {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine into the hot pan and let it bubble,

the fond, for 1–2 minutes until reduced by half. Add the remaining 30g of butter and swirl the pan until it melts into the wine. Remove the cocktail sticks from the escalopes. Spoon the sauce over and serve at once.

Chicken breast escalopes work well in place of veal — cut each breast horizontally and beat flat. Pork escalopes (from the loin) are the closest in texture and flavour. The cooking time increases by 1–2 minutes per side. Everything else in the recipe stays the same.
